config.title: Altes Museum Berlin FAQ
config.subtitle: Quick answers about Schinkel’s building, tickets, opening hours and whether the Altes Museum is worth visiting.

items.q: What is the Altes Museum in Berlin?
items.a: **The Altes Museum is Berlin’s first museum building on Museum Island.** It opened in 1830 and now houses the Antikensammlung, the Collection of Classical Antiquities.
**Karl Friedrich Schinkel designed the Altes Museum.** It is one of Berlin’s major Neoclassical buildings, with 18 Ionic columns and a rotunda inspired by the Pantheon in Rome.

items.q: What can I see inside the Altes Museum?
items.a: The permanent display covers **Greeks, Etruscans and Romans**, with sculpture, vases, Roman portraits, ancient coins and highlights such as the Praying Boy and the Berlin Goddess.

items.q: How much is the Altes Museum ticket?
items.a: As of the checked 2026 official listing, the Altes Museum ticket is **EUR 14**, reduced **EUR 7**. The Museum Island day ticket is **EUR 24**, reduced **EUR 12**.

items.q: Is the Altes Museum open on Mondays?
items.a: No. The current public listing shows **Monday closed**, Tuesday to Friday **10:00 to 17:00**, and Saturday to Sunday **10:00 to 18:00**. Always re-check before visiting.

items.q: Is the Altes Museum worth visiting?
items.a: Yes, if you like classical architecture or ancient objects. If you only want Nefertiti or big archaeology spectacles, the Neues Museum or Pergamon-related displays may fit better.
